GLM-5.2 B300 CUTLASS Kernel Study
PP2 tree H200 vs B300 Timeline Events All events CSV

One request · CUTLASS MoE · eight B300 pipeline ranks

Basic Kernel Understanding First — B300 CUTLASS

Every CUDA kernel launched during one uncached 16,384-token GLM-5.2 request with --moe-runner-backend cutlass, aligned to request start and separated by PP rank.

Contents

Overview Run contract Experiment settings PP ranks PP2 execution tree H200 vs B300 Launch timeline Kernel families Event explorer Evidence

Overview

This is a structure-first CUTLASS trace rendered with the same rank, phase, layer, module-tree, precision, family, and event views as the H200 report, enabling a like-for-like PP2 comparison.

Primary interpretation

Run Contract

The measured region contains one user request only. Server load, JIT compilation, built-in warmup, and health checks finished before profiler collection was enabled.

CUTLASS is the assumed primary backend Every timeline, phase, layer, kernel table, and MFU value on this redraw comes from the profiled --moe-runner-backend cutlass request—not from the earlier default TRT-LLM MoE trace.
  • The model is the validated zai-org/GLM-5.2-FP8 revision on eight NVIDIA B300 SXM6 AC GPUs.
  • The request uses TP=1, DP=1, PP=8, 16,384 uncached input tokens, and exactly one generated token.
  • The output text hash matches every completed A/B variant; this is a deterministic regression gate, not a model-quality evaluation.

Exact Reproduction Settings

The page visualizes the final profiled request from a same-node backend experiment. The profiler contains one request, while the complete CUTLASS variant also includes the warm-up and unprofiled repetitions shown below.

ScopeRecorded settingReproduction meaning
Run and hardware Hebei machine 1; node gpu-b300-103; 8 × NVIDIA B300 SXM6 AC; driver 580.126.20. The GPU inventory recorded a 1,100 W power limit and observed clocks, but the experiment did not lock application clocks. Do not treat the observed inventory clocks as a fixed clock contract.
Model zai-org/GLM-5.2-FP8
revision ba978f7d347eaf65d22f1a86833408afdb953541		 The revision was verified before the sequential A/B run; keep it pinned rather than relying on the repository head.
Runtime image.hpc-ai.com/platform/dynamo-sglang-runtime:57408ea1-joint-v0.5.14-v1.2.1-20260714184447
digest sha256:8fa3a4c397860756070300e9adab901abd0098a34ac7bfff13ae0be20b789f8b
SGLang 3bd16074e02da069acdc959f0968af1d63fb9467; CUDA 13; NCCL 2.28.9+cuda13.0.		 The digest, not only the mutable tag, is the image identity.
Topology One node; TP=1, DP=1, EP=1, PP=8; layer partition [9, 9, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10]. One pipeline stage maps to each GPU. The report's PP2 accounting covers layers 18–27.
Backend and capacity CUTLASS MoE; context 1,048,576; chunk/max prefill 16,384; max running 48; static-memory fraction 0.85; FP8-E4M3 KV; page size 64; decode and piecewise CUDA graphs disabled. The one backend delta is --moe-runner-backend cutlass; the remaining settings are held fixed across the A/B variants.
Sequence and cache Built-in server warm-up, then 2 user warm-ups, 7 unprofiled measurements, and 1 separately profiled request. /flush_cache ran before every user request. The flush cleared request radix/KV-prefix state; mounted JIT, Triton, and DeepGEMM compilation caches remained warm. Therefore “uncached” means zero reused prompt tokens, not a cold compilation cache.
Transfer mode Standalone prefill: disaggregation_mode=null, EP backend none, hierarchical cache disabled. The resolved default transfer-backend field says mooncake, but it is inactive when disaggregation mode is null. No PD or hierarchical-cache transfer is measured.

Server launch contract

python3 -m sglang.launch_server \
  --model-path /home/models/zai-org/GLM-5.2-FP8 \
  --served-model-name zai-org/glm-5.2 \
  --trust-remote-code --host 0.0.0.0 --port 30000 \
  --tp-size 1 --dp-size 1 --pp-size 8 \
  --enable-fused-qk-norm-rope \
  --tool-call-parser glm47 --reasoning-parser glm45 \
  --context-length 1048576 \
  --chunked-prefill-size 16384 --max-prefill-tokens 16384 \
  --max-running-requests 48 --mem-fraction-static 0.85 \
  --kv-cache-dtype fp8_e4m3 --page-size 64 \
  --enable-dynamic-batch-tokenizer --enable-metrics \
  --enable-cache-report --disable-decode-cuda-graph \
  --disable-piecewise-cuda-graph \
  --soft-watchdog-timeout 7200 --watchdog-timeout 7200 \
  --decode-log-interval 1 --log-requests \
  --log-requests-level 1 --log-requests-target stdout \
  --moe-runner-backend cutlass

Request and measurement contract

input_ids = [1000 + (i % 1024) for i in range(16384)]
canonical compact-JSON SHA-256 =
8cbff7c802f11954db30888442ddf2b4636ffd876bcb3640d8ef0970aee7d25f

POST /flush_cache {}
POST /generate {
  "input_ids": input_ids,
  "sampling_params": {
    "temperature": 0,
    "max_new_tokens": 1,
    "ignore_eos": true
  },
  "stream": false
}

2 warm-ups + 7 measured + 1 profiled
required: HTTP 200, prompt=16384, completion=1, cached=0

Profiler contract

nsys profile \
  --capture-range=cudaProfilerApi \
  --capture-range-end=stop-shutdown --kill=sigterm \
  --trace=cuda,nvtx,osrt --cuda-graph-trace=node \
  --sample=none --cpuctxsw=none \
  --force-overwrite=true --export=sqlite

POST /flush_cache {}
POST /start_profile {
  "activities": ["CUDA_PROFILER"],
  "with_stack": false,
  "record_shapes": false
}
POST /generate → persist result
POST /stop_profile {"activities": ["CUDA_PROFILER"]}

Pod, JIT, cache, and resource boundary

FLASHINFER_NVCC_THREADS=16
MAX_JOBS=16
SGLANG_ENABLE_JIT_DEEPGEMM=1
SGLANG_JIT_DEEPGEMM_COMPILE_WORKERS=32
SGLANG_JIT_DEEPGEMM_FAST_WARMUP=1
SGLANG_WARMUP_TIMEOUT=3600
NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0
GLOO_SOCKET_IFNAME=bond0

requests: cpu=16, memory=1200Gi, ephemeral=64Gi, gpu=8
limits:   cpu=32, memory=1500Gi, ephemeral=512Gi, gpu=8
security: privileged root; SYS_PTRACE + IPC_LOCK; host network
model:    /mnt/disk1/models → /home/models (read-only)
cache:    /mnt/disk1/codex-cache/glm52-fp8-profile → /root/.cache
profiles: /mnt/disk1/codex-profiles/b300_fp8_kernel_mfu_opt_20260722 → /profiles
tmp:      64Gi emptyDir
shm:      64Gi memory-backed emptyDir
Provenance, retention, and interpretation limits Run b300_fp8_kernel_mfu_opt_20260722/moe_cutlass is the primary experiment. The retained source SQLite has SHA-256 67c38df7f6dd3802c0001f3ed993eaf1bd6d516974252e1f54ec4f762880c64c; normalized JSON/CSV and audit sidecars are downloadable below. The full .nsys-rep is not part of this published pack, so its continued availability is not asserted. MFU uses analytical model FLOPs and a 4.5-PFLOP/s-per-GPU dense-FP8 normalization, not NCU counters. Aggregate kernel milliseconds sum device residency and are neither request wall time nor useful-compute time. The H200 comparison is a separate evidence-pack dependency with its own precision conversion.

PP Rank Waterfall

Device 0 maps to PP0, through device 7 mapping to PP7. The process names and GPU UUIDs were captured before collection.

Per-rank launch accounting

RankLayer rangeLayersFull indexersLaunchesModel startModel finishModel windowModel kernel msNCCL ms

PP2: From 497 CUTLASS Launches To Model Modules

PP2 owns layers 18-27. This section transplants the decoder-stack structure from the GLM-5.2 execution tree and reconciles it with this request's actual Nsight launch order.

Phase reconciliation

PP2 phasePP2 event ordinalsLaunchesAggregate kernel msTrace evidence

Ten-layer motif ledger

A CUTLASS reuse layer has the 42-launch base motif. A selector-refresh layer adds exactly 16 launches, producing the 58-launch motif.

LayerSelectorPP2 event ordinalsLaunchesAggregate kernel msWall span msMax launchless gap ms
The CUTLASS motif is stable across all ten layers Every selector-refresh layer contains 58 launches and every selector-reuse layer contains 42. The semantic decoder totals exactly 468 launches and 159.021 aggregate kernel ms.

Decoder execution tree

Inclusive subtotal Selector refresh Selector reuse Sparse MoE FP8 GEMM ×1 BF16/FP16 GEMM or attention MMA ×2 TF32 MMA ×4 FP32/SFU work remains separate
    Alignment boundary

    H200 Versus B300 CUTLASS: Same PP2 Accounting

    Both columns use the same GLM-5.2 module boundaries, additive leaves, and FP8-equivalent rules as the original H200 PP2 tree. Each hardware column uses its own dense FP8 peak. The B300 trace and tree assume --moe-runner-backend cutlass throughout.

    H200 normalized MFU
    34.606%
    251.534 TFLOP-eq ÷ (367.277 ms × 1,979 TFLOP/s)
    B300 CUTLASS normalized MFU
    29.028%
    207.723 TFLOP-eq ÷ (159.021 ms × 4,500 TFLOP/s)
    B300 elapsed speedup
    2.310×
    367.277 ms ÷ 159.021 ms
    B300/H200 FP8 peak
    2.274×
    4,500 ÷ 1,979 TFLOP/s
    Equivalent-work difference
    −17.417%
    B300 attention is FP8 ×1; H200 attention is BF16 ×2
    Support-time share
    19.939%
    B300 versus 11.583% on H200
    Why faster B300 still reports lower MFU B300 shortens the decoder by 56.703%, but its FP8 peak denominator is 2.274× larger and this trace carries 17.417% less FP8-equivalent work because its sparse attention MMA is E4M3 rather than H200's BF16 path. The CUTLASS grouped runner also changes the MoE motif from 20 to 27 launches per layer, making grouping, shuffle, activation, and combine work a larger fraction of the shorter B300 decoder.

    Additive classified leaves: direct efficiency comparison

    “Extra B300 ms” is a diagnostic counterfactual: how much longer the B300 leaf took than it would have taken at the H200 leaf's normalized MFU, using B300's 4,500-TFLOP/s peak and B300's own FP8-equivalent work. Positive values identify drag; the rows are sorted by this value. They are additive leaves, so their times do not double count.

    OperationH200 modeH200 ms H200 MFUB300 mode B300 msB300 MFU MFU ΔB300 time share Extra B300 ms
    M3b
    Active-expert gate/up projections    		FP8 GEMM95.76739.160%CUTLASS grouped FP8 GEMM49.07333.609%-5.551 pp30.859%+6.956
    2.1.7
    Selected sparse MLA scan    		BF16 attention MMA; FP32/SFU work separate128.86834.357%E4M3 Q/K/V attention MMA; FP32/SFU work separate31.94630.475%-3.882 pp20.089%+3.610
    M3d
    Active-expert down projections    		FP8 GEMM58.60731.995%CUTLASS grouped FP8 GEMM27.78229.683%-2.312 pp17.470%+2.008
    2.1.2
    fused_qkv_a_proj_with_mqa    		FP8 GEMM4.74956.206%FP8 GEMM2.28651.358%-4.848 pp1.437%+0.197
    A2
    Causal candidate scoring    		FP8 GEMM3.68145.636%FP8 GEMM1.73842.514%-3.122 pp1.093%+0.119
    2.1.9
    o_proj    		FP8 GEMM24.59267.776%FP8 GEMM10.93067.063%-0.713 pp6.873%+0.115
    2.1.4
    q_b_proj    		FP8 GEMM8.47165.590%FP8 GEMM3.55968.648%+3.058 pp2.238%-0.166
    Largest classified drags Gate/up expert projection contributes +6.956 ms, selected sparse MLA contributes +3.610 ms, and expert down projection contributes +2.008 ms versus the corresponding H200 leaf efficiencies. Separately, support and unconverted leaves occupy 31.708 ms (19.939%) on B300 versus 42.542 ms (11.583%) on H200; these kernels consume denominator time but are not assigned Tensor-Core FLOPs.

    Kernel Launch Timeline

    Each mark is one recorded kernel execution on one rank. Every PP rank is split into logical Compute and Communication stripes; the tooltip preserves the physical CUDA stream ID. Horizontal position is launch time relative to the client beginning the single /generate request, and mark width is event duration.

    PP0–PP7 compute / communication lanes

    Non-NCCL kernels use Compute; NCCL kernels use Communication. The vertical red line is t = 0.

    One-millisecond kernel time density

    Expanded kernel family density

    Aggregate event-interval overlap across eight devices in each 1 ms bin.

    The simple eight-device baseline is 8 GPU-ms per 1 ms wall-clock bin. Aggregate event overlap can exceed that baseline; this is residency accounting, not utilization or useful FLOPs.

    Kernel Families And Names

    Families make the sequence readable; raw demangled names remain available in the tables and CSV so every grouping can be audited.

    FamilyLaunchesAggregate msAverage µsRepresentative names

    Top kernels by aggregate event time

    #FamilyLaunchesTotal msAverage µsMin µsMax µsKernel

    Top names within each PP rank

    #FamilyLaunchesTotal msAverage µsKernel

    Event Explorer

    The launch offset is stored as an exact integer nanosecond delta from request start, avoiding unsafe absolute nanosecond values in the browser.

    #RankLaunch offset nsLaunch offset msDuration µsFamilyKernel

    Evidence And Limitations

    The page keeps request, scheduler, profiler, and launch-count semantics separate so a post-request idle tail cannot be mistaken for request work.
